title: SpatialData
text:
In this demo, we’ll be analyzing 5K-plex Xenium (10x Genomics) data on a tissue section from a human breast cancer biopsy, derived from here. These include the following elements:

two images (histology and immunofluorescence)
two shapes (nuclei and membrane boundaries)
one table (gene \(\times\) cell data)
one point (RNA transcripts)

A size-reduced example dataset has been deposited on Zenodo. We’ll first retrieve the compressed .zarr store programatically, decompress it, and stash the data’s on-disk file path for later:

# retrieve .zarr store from the web
url <- "https://zenodo.org/records/20083116/files/BC_xenium_sdata_lowres.zarr.zip?download=1"
zip <- tempfile("sd", fileext=".zarr.zip")
download.file(url, zip, quiet=TRUE)
# decompress, stash & view contents
nm <- unzip(zip, exdir=dirname(zip))
zs <- grepv("zarr$", dirname(nm))
cat(basename(zs))

BC_xenium_sdata_lowres.zarr




├── images
│   ├── he_image
│   │   ├── 0
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   ├── morphology_focus
│   │   ├── 0
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   └── zarr.json
├── points
│   ├── transcripts
│   │   ├── points.parquet
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   └── zarr.json
├── shapes
│   ├── cell_boundaries
│   │   ├── shapes.parquet
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   ├── nucleus_boundaries
│   │   ├── shapes.parquet
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   └── zarr.json
├── tables
│   ├── table
│   │   ├── X
│   │   ├── layers
│   │   ├── obs
│   │   ├── obsm
│   │   ├── obsp
│   │   ├── raw
│   │   ├── uns
│   │   ├── var
│   │   ├── varm
│   │   ├── varp
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   └── zarr.json
└── zarr.json





library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(anndataR)
library(scrapper)
library(patchwork)
library(reticulate)
library(SpatialData)
library(SpatialData.data)
library(SpatialData.plot)
library(SingleCellExperiment)




We can instruct our session to use an existing environment with our Python dependencies (here, SegTraQ) as follows. Note that reticulate provides similar commands to, e.g., use a virtual (not conda) environemnt instead:


The environment used here has been set up previously outside of R, from the command line, using these commands:
conda create -n segtraq python=3.11
conda activate segtraq
pip install segtraq

# path to conda binary
bin <- "~/software/mambaforge/bin/conda" 
options(reticulate.conda_binary=bin)
# activate environment
use_condaenv("segtraq")





import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings(action="ignore")

import anndata as ad
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import segtraq
import spatialdata as sd
import spatialdata_plot

sdata = sd.read_zarr(r.zs)

sdata.shapes["cell_boundaries"].index.name = "cell_id"
sdata.shapes["nucleus_boundaries"].index.name = "cell_id"

st = segtraq.SegTraQ(sdata,
    images_key="morphology_focus", 
    tables_centroid_x_key=None, 
    tables_centroid_y_key=None)
    
st.run_baseline()

adata = st.sdata.tables["table"]
adata


axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 5), constrained_layout=True)[1].flatten()

# plot DAPI image
a = st.sdata.pl.render_images("morphology_focus", channel="DAPI")
a.pl.show(ax=axes[0], title="DAPI", coordinate_systems="global")

# plot histology
b = st.sdata.pl.render_images("he_image")
b.pl.show(ax=axes[1], title="H&E", coordinate_systems="global")

# plot overlay of nuclei & cell boundaries colored by cell area
c = st.sdata.pl.render_shapes(
    element="nucleus_boundaries",
    outline_color="black")
c = c.pl.render_shapes(
    element="cell_boundaries",
    outline_color="black")
c.pl.show(ax=axes[2], title="overlay", colorbar=True)

plt.show()




Let’s load up the data in R, skipping the table layer. We then retrieve the table, including SegTraQ results, from Python, and set it to annotate cell segmentation boundaries using setTable(); ik specifies the spatialdata’s instance_key used to link observations between table and spatial element.

sd <- readSpatialData(zs)#, tables=FALSE)
# sd <- setTable(sd, 
#     i="cell_boundaries", ik="cell_id",
#     y=py$adata$as_SingleCellExperiment())
sd

class: SpatialData
- images(2):
  - he_image (3,1399,1649)
  - morphology_focus (4,2111,1787)
- labels(0):
- points(1):
  - transcripts (2978451)
- shapes(2):
  - cell_boundaries (18644,circle)
  - nucleus_boundaries (18407,circle)
- tables(1):
  - table (5101,18644) [cell_boundaries]
coordinate systems(1):
- global(5): he_image morphology_focus cell_boundaries
  nucleus_boundaries transcripts




Let’s start out by visualization the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. H&E provides a high-contrast visualization of tissue microarchitecture and cellular morphology through differential staining of nuclear and cytoplasmic components. To date, whole-slide H&E images (WSIs) remain the clinical gold standard for cancer diagnosis, including tumor grading, staging, and prognosis.

plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sd)








We can also zoom into a particular region by specifying a bounding box and using crop() to subset the data accordingly; to do this more efficiently, we subset the object to contain only images before cropping:

bb <- list(
    xmin=2000, xmax=4000, 
    ymin=2000, ymax=3000)
sp <- crop(sd["images", ], bb)
plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sp) 











In addition to the H&E, the dataset includes 4 immunofluorescence (IF) markers:

DAPI (nuclei) marks all cell nuclei and is used for cell segmentation
ATP1A1/CD45/E-Cadherin (membrane) marks cell boundaries and major lineages
18S (cytoplasm) marks the cell body/cytoplasmic RNA and overall cell extent
αSMA/Vimentin (stroma) marks mesenchymal/stromal tissue, including fibroblasts


img <- image(sd, 2)
as.vector(channels(img))

[1] "DAPI"                   "ATP1A1/CD45/E-Cadherin" "18S"                   
[4] "AlphaSMA/Vimentin"     


The underlying image is thus a 3D array where the first dimension are channels, and the second and third dimensions are height and width; we can verify this by viewing the axes() specified in the .zattrs associated with the image:

do.call(rbind, axes(img))

     name type     
[1,] "c"  "channel"
[2,] "y"  "space"  
[3,] "x"  "space"  

cat(dim(img))

4 2111 1787


Let’s visualize the four-plex composite image overlaying these markers; for consistent visualization, we also specify colors to use for each channel:

pal <- c("blue", "green", "cyan", "magenta")
chs <- names(pal) <- channels(img)
plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sd, 2, ch=chs, c=pal[chs])








Alternatively, we can visualize each channel separately; here, using the previously smaller region:

ps <- lapply(chs, \(.) plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sp, 2, ch=., c=pal[.]) + ggtitle(.))
wrap_plots(ps, nrow=2) & theme_void() & theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5), legend.position="none")








We can also zoom in further to inspect how well cell segmentation boundaries align with the immunofluorescence signals used to infer them:

# sq <- crop(sd, list(xmin=2500, xmax=3000, ymin=2000, ymax=2500))
# plotSpatialData() +
#     plotImage(sq, 2, ch=chs, c=pal[chs]) +
#     plotShape(sq, fill=NA, col="white", linewidth=1)





The SpatialData object contains a single table element – represented as a SingleCellExperiment – annotating cell_boundaries. This table contains the single-cell level data: a gene \(\times\) cell count matrix (assay slot "X"), gene/cell metadata (row/colData), as well as spatial coordinates (reducedDim slot "spatial").

(se <- tables(sd)[[1]])

class: SingleCellExperiment 
dim: 5101 18644 
metadata(0):
assays(1): X
rownames(5101): A2ML1 AAMP ... ZYG11B ZYX
rowData names(3): gene_ids feature_types genome
colnames(18644): 6430 6431 ... 687177 687178
colData names(12): transcript_counts control_probe_counts ...
  segmentation_method z_level
reducedDimNames(1): spatial
mainExpName: NULL
altExpNames(0):


Note that these data originally stem from a Python anndata object written to .zarr, which were read into R using anndataR. We can therefore perform standard processing tasks using tools available in R/Bioconductor, e.g., scrapper, including:

cell-level quality control
log-library size normalization
highly variable gene (HVG) selection
principal component analysis (PCA)
shared-nearest neighbor (SNN) graph construction
community detection using the Leiden algorithm


se <- tables(sd)[[1]]
assayNames(se) <- "counts"
se <- quickRnaQc.se(se, list())
cpa <- log(se$sum / se$cell_area)
ex <- cpa < median(cpa)-2*mad(cpa)
se$ex <- ex | !se$keep



Code
# bug? shapes are missing this...
shape(sd) <- shape(sd) |> mutate(cell_id=`__index_level_0__`)
shape(sd)@meta$spatialdata_attrs$instance_key <- "cell_id"

sp <- sd
table(sp) <- se
plotSpatialData() + 
    plotShape(sp, col=NA, fill="ex") + 
    scale_fill_manual("exclude", values=c("grey", "red"))










se <- se[, !ex]
se <- normalizeRnaCounts.se(se)
se <- chooseRnaHvgs.se(se)
se <- runPca.se(se, features=rowData(se)$hvg)
se <- clusterGraph.se(se, method="leiden", resolution=0.5)
nk <- nlevels(se$clusters)

For convenience, we’ll add a few principal components (PCs) as cell metadata:

npc <- seq_len(3)
pcs <- reducedDim(se, "PCA")[, npc]
colnames(pcs) <- paste0("PC", npc)
colData(se) <- cbind(colData(se), pcs)

Lastly, we’ll overwrite the object’s original table with the processed one:

# TODO: this needs fixing
table(sd) <- se
shape(sd) <- shape(sd)[match(instances(se), instances(shape(sd)), nomatch=0)] 


plotSpatialData() + 
    plotShape(sd, col=NA, fill="clusters") +
    scale_fill_manual(values=hcl.colors(nk, "Spectral")) 







library(sosta)
library(spatstat.geom)


xy <- centroids(SpatialData::transform(shape(sd)))
ow <- owin(xrange=range(xy[, 1]), yrange=range(xy[, 2]))
pp <- ppp(x=xy[, 1], y=xy[, 2], window=ow, marks=se$clusters)

For now the sosta output is not yet stored in the object; we’ll plot it using geom_sf(). For illustration, we’ll highlight the largest regions:

roi <- reconstructShapeDensity(pp, markSelect=8, dim=400)
roj <- roi[head(order(st_area(roi), decreasing=TRUE), n=20), ]

plotSpatialData() + 
    plotShape(sd, col=NA, fill="clusters") + 
    geom_sf(data=roj, col="red", fill=NA, linewidth=1) +
    scale_fill_manual(values=hcl.colors(nk, "Spectral")) 


















session






sessionInfo()

title: SpatialData
text:
In this demo, we’ll be analyzing 5K-plex Xenium (10x Genomics) data on a tissue section from a human breast cancer biopsy, derived from here. These include the following elements:

two images (histology and immunofluorescence)
two shapes (nuclei and membrane boundaries)
one table (gene \(\times\) cell data)
one point (RNA transcripts)

A size-reduced example dataset has been deposited on Zenodo. We’ll first retrieve the compressed .zarr store programatically, decompress it, and stash the data’s on-disk file path for later:

# retrieve .zarr store from the web
url <- "https://zenodo.org/records/20083116/files/BC_xenium_sdata_lowres.zarr.zip?download=1"
zip <- tempfile("sd", fileext=".zarr.zip")
download.file(url, zip, quiet=TRUE)
# decompress, stash & view contents
nm <- unzip(zip, exdir=dirname(zip))
zs <- grepv("zarr$", dirname(nm))
cat(basename(zs))

BC_xenium_sdata_lowres.zarr




├── images
│   ├── he_image
│   │   ├── 0
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   ├── morphology_focus
│   │   ├── 0
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   └── zarr.json
├── points
│   ├── transcripts
│   │   ├── points.parquet
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   └── zarr.json
├── shapes
│   ├── cell_boundaries
│   │   ├── shapes.parquet
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   ├── nucleus_boundaries
│   │   ├── shapes.parquet
│   │   └── zarr.json
│   └── zarr.json
├── tables
│   ├── table
│   │   ├── X
│   │   ├── layers

└── zarr.json





library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(anndataR)
library(scrapper)
library(patchwork)
library(reticulate)
library(SpatialData)
library(SpatialData.data)
library(SpatialData.plot)
library(SingleCellExperiment)




We can instruct our session to use an existing environment with our Python dependencies (here, SegTraQ) as follows. Note that reticulate provides similar commands to, e.g., use a virtual (not conda) environemnt instead:


The environment used here has been set up previously outside of R, from the command line, using these commands:
conda create -n segtraq python=3.11
conda activate segtraq
pip install segtraq

# path to conda binary
bin <- "~/software/mambaforge/bin/conda" 
options(reticulate.conda_binary=bin)
# activate environment
use_condaenv("segtraq")





import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings(action="ignore")

import anndata as ad
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import segtraq
import spatialdata as sd
import spatialdata_plot

sdata = sd.read_zarr(r.zs)

sdata.shapes["cell_boundaries"].index.name = "cell_id"
sdata.shapes["nucleus_boundaries"].index.name = "cell_id"

st = segtraq.SegTraQ(sdata,
    images_key="morphology_focus", 
    tables_centroid_x_key=None, 
    tables_centroid_y_key=None)
    
st.run_baseline()

adata = st.sdata.tables["table"]
adata


axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 5), constrained_layout=True)[1].flatten()

# plot DAPI image
a = st.sdata.pl.render_images("morphology_focus", channel="DAPI")
a.pl.show(ax=axes[0], title="DAPI", coordinate_systems="global")

# plot histology
b = st.sdata.pl.render_images("he_image")
b.pl.show(ax=axes[1], title="H&E", coordinate_systems="global")

# plot overlay of nuclei & cell boundaries colored by cell area
c = st.sdata.pl.render_shapes(
    element="nucleus_boundaries",
    outline_color="black")
c = c.pl.render_shapes(
    element="cell_boundaries",
    outline_color="black")
c.pl.show(ax=axes[2], title="overlay", colorbar=True)

plt.show()




Let’s load up the data in R, skipping the table layer. We then retrieve the table, including SegTraQ results, from Python, and set it to annotate cell segmentation boundaries using setTable(); ik specifies the spatialdata’s instance_key used to link observations between table and spatial element.

sd <- readSpatialData(zs)#, tables=FALSE)
# sd <- setTable(sd, 
#     i="cell_boundaries", ik="cell_id",
#     y=py$adata$as_SingleCellExperiment())
sd

- images(2):
  - he_image (3,1399,1649)
  - morphology_focus (4,2111,1787)
- labels(0):
- points(1):

coordinate systems(1):

  nucleus_boundaries transcripts

H&E provides a high-contrast visualization of tissue microarchitecture and cellular morphology through differential staining of nuclear and cytoplasmic components. plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sd)

bb <- list(
    xmin=2000, xmax=4000, 
    ymin=2000, ymax=3000)
sp <- crop(sd["images", ], bb)
plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sp)

DAPI (nuclei) marks all cell nuclei and is used for cell segmentation
ATP1A1/CD45/E-Cadherin (membrane) marks cell boundaries and major lineages

αSMA/Vimentin (stroma) marks mesenchymal/stromal tissue, including fibroblasts


img <- image(sd, 2)
as.vector(channels(img))

[1] "DAPI"                   "ATP1A1/CD45/E-Cadherin" "18S"                   
[4] "AlphaSMA/Vimentin"     


The underlying image is thus a 3D array where the first dimension are channels, and the second and third dimensions are height and width; we can verify this by viewing the axes() specified in the .zattrs associated with the image:

do.call(rbind, axes(img))

     name type     
[1,] "c"  "channel"
[2,] "y"  "space"  
[3,] "x"  "space"  

cat(dim(img))

4 2111 1787


Let’s visualize the four-plex composite image overlaying these markers; for consistent visualization, we also specify colors to use for each channel:

pal <- c("blue", "green", "cyan", "magenta")
chs <- names(pal) <- channels(img)
plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sd, 2, ch=chs, c=pal[chs])








Alternatively, we can visualize each channel separately; here, using the previously smaller region:

ps <- lapply(chs, \(.) plotSpatialData() + plotImage(sp, 2, ch=., c=pal[.]) + ggtitle(.))
wrap_plots(ps, nrow=2) & theme_void() & theme(plot.title=element_text(hjust=0.5), legend.position="none")








We can also zoom in further to inspect how well cell segmentation boundaries align with the immunofluorescence signals used to infer them:

# sq <- crop(sd, list(xmin=2500, xmax=3000, ymin=2000, ymax=2500))
# plotSpatialData() +
#     plotImage(sq, 2, ch=chs, c=pal[chs]) +
#     plotShape(sq, fill=NA, col="white", linewidth=1)





The SpatialData object contains a single table element – represented as a SingleCellExperiment – annotating cell_boundaries. This table contains the single-cell level data: a gene \(\times\) cell count matrix (assay slot "X"), gene/cell metadata (row/colData), as well as spatial coordinates (reducedDim slot "spatial").

(se <- tables(sd)[[1]])

class: SingleCellExperiment 
dim: 5101 18644 
metadata(0):
assays(1): X
rownames(5101): A2ML1 AAMP ... ZYG11B ZYX
rowData names(3): gene_ids feature_types genome
colnames(18644): 6430 6431 ... 687177 687178
colData names(12): transcript_counts control_probe_counts ...
  segmentation_method z_level
reducedDimNames(1): spatial
mainExpName: NULL

Note that these data originally stem from a Python anndata object written to .zarr, which were read into R using anndataR. We can therefore perform standard processing tasks using tools available in R/Bioconductor, e.g., scrapper, including:

cell-level quality control
log-library size normalization
highly variable gene (HVG) selection
principal component analysis (PCA)
shared-nearest neighbor (SNN) graph construction
community detection using the Leiden algorithm


se <- tables(sd)[[1]]
assayNames(se) <- "counts"
se <- quickRnaQc.se(se, list())
cpa <- log(se$sum / se$cell_area)
ex <- cpa < median(cpa)-2*mad(cpa)
se$ex <- ex | !se$keep



Code
# bug? shapes are missing this...
shape(sd) <- shape(sd) |> mutate(cell_id=`__index_level_0__`)
shape(sd)@meta$spatialdata_attrs$instance_key <- "cell_id"

sp <- sd
table(sp) <- se
plotSpatialData() + 
    plotShape(sp, col=NA, fill="ex") + 
    scale_fill_manual("exclude", values=c("grey", "red"))










se <- se[, !ex]
se <- normalizeRnaCounts.se(se)
se <- chooseRnaHvgs.se(se)
se <- runPca.se(se, features=rowData(se)$hvg)
se <- clusterGraph.se(se, method="leiden", resolution=0.5)
nk <- nlevels(se$clusters)

For convenience, we’ll add a few principal components (PCs) as cell metadata:

npc <- seq_len(3)
pcs <- reducedDim(se, "PCA")[, npc]
colnames(pcs) <- paste0("PC", npc)
colData(se) <- cbind(colData(se), pcs)

Lastly, we’ll overwrite the object’s original table with the processed one:

# TODO: this needs fixing
table(sd) <- se
shape(sd) <- shape(sd)[match(instances(se), instances(shape(sd)), nomatch=0)] 


plotSpatialData() + 
    plotShape(sd, col=NA, fill="clusters") +
    scale_fill_manual(values=hcl.colors(nk, "Spectral")) 







library(sosta)
library(spatstat.geom)


xy <- centroids(SpatialData::transform(shape(sd)))
ow <- owin(xrange=range(xy[, 1]), yrange=range(xy[, 2]))
pp <- ppp(x=xy[, 1], y=xy[, 2], window=ow, marks=se$clusters)

For now the sosta output is not yet stored in the object; we’ll plot it using geom_sf(). For illustration, we’ll highlight the largest regions:

roi <- reconstructShapeDensity(pp, markSelect=8, dim=400)
roj <- roi[head(order(st_area(roi), decreasing=TRUE), n=20), ]

plotSpatialData() + 
    plotShape(sd, col=NA, fill="clusters") + 
    geom_sf(data=roj, col="red", fill=NA, linewidth=1) +
    scale_fill_manual(values=hcl.colors(nk, "Spectral"))
